guest_role: New Venture Partner, First Round Capital
assertiveness_why: Harry demonstrates occasional direct challenges, especially on early-stage LTV/CAC metrics, and plants his own thesis, but remains mostly in receiving mode without sustained pressure-testing.
question_style: Harry asks prepared, multi-sentence open-ended questions with real follow-ups. He pushes back directly on pre-seed metrics focus and expresses frustration. Conversational tone throughout; minimal interruptions; mostly allows guests full responses without contestation.
tone: Warm and matey throughout, using casual language like 'my word, chaps.' Genuinely excited about the announcement. Self-deprecating during sponsor reads. Shows clear admiration for Finn and Josh. Conversational energy, friendly fandom balanced with professional inquiry.
depth: Strong strategic follow-ups on product philosophy (Gmail's Consumer UI Review process, 30-40ms delay scrutiny), business model evolution (bottom-up SaaS, LTV:CAC ratios at scale), founder DNA and culture. Explores product-market fit framework in detail. Asks about coaching methodology and transition from angel to institutional investing. Follows the arc from product to organization building.
harry_quotes: How did you make your way into the world of venture then as of the news today?
What advice would you give to Todd sitting around this fire with Amaretto in hand?
What would they be and how do you think they impacted your mindset?
What are your thoughts on product market fit today?
Todd, what does it take to be a great coach to founders, do you think?
How do you think your mindset will shift with the change from angel to institutional investing?
learnings: Building high-quality products demands rigorous design review processes and obsessive attention to performance, even microsecond-level delays warrant scrapping and restarting implementation.
Successful scaling requires aligning business model to product; Dropbox's shift from consumer to bottom-up SaaS unlocked efficient growth and venture-scale revenue predictability.
Company culture and decision-making patterns naturally reflect founder values and should be intentionally codified and communicated to align with market demands.
At pre-seed/seed stages, investors should assess founder orientation toward metrics rather than demand precise LTV/CAC predictions.
Product-market fit emerges from addressing both functional needs (why users try) and emotional needs (why they share), in large underserved markets.
The single most important founder decision is selecting the right market, as it constrains all other choices.
Effective angel coaching requires listening and guiding founders through their own problem-solving rather than imposing solutions.
VC firms avoid generational transition failures by adopting team-based decision-making without individual portfolio attribution, prioritizing firm franchise over personal scorecards.
repeat_guest_callback: If you haven't listened to Finn's prior episode, then that really is a must.
notable: First 20VC episode ever to feature two simultaneous guests; announcement-driven format celebrating Todd Jackson's transition to Venture Partner at First Round Capital.
